title: Sad men should eat soup
summary: A London journalist spends ten days at a Croatian lighthouse to write about the keeper. He ends up writing about the cook.
body:
My name is Tom. I am a journalist from London. Last winter I spent ten days at a small lighthouse on a Croatian island. The lighthouse has a keeper, an electrician, and a cook. I went to write about the keeper. I ended up writing about the cook.

The cook is a woman named Ivana. She is fifty-three. She has been the cook at the lighthouse for sixteen years. She comes by boat on Mondays. She leaves on Saturdays. She cooks for three.

On my first night, dinner was fish soup, bread, cheese, and one glass of wine. On the second night, the same. On the third night, the same. I asked Ivana if she ever cooks something different.

She smiled. She said, 'The men ask me to cook fish soup. I cook fish soup. Sometimes the keeper asks for pasta. I cook pasta. Sometimes the electrician asks for nothing — he is sad — and I cook fish soup anyway. Sad men should eat soup.'

I said, 'And for yourself?'

She said, 'I eat the same. If I cook two meals, I am a tired woman. If I cook one meal, I am a cook.'

On the tenth day, I left. The keeper saw me off. The electrician waved. Ivana was in the kitchen. She did not come out. The soup was on the stove.

annotations.meaningVi: cuối cùng lại viết về — 'end up' + V-ing = kết thúc bằng việc làm gì đó ngoài dự định
tiễn tôi (ra đi) — cụm động từ: see someone off = ra tiễn ai lúc họ rời đi
dù sao / dù thế nào — dùng để nhấn mạnh rằng điều gì đó xảy ra bất chấp hoàn cảnh
đến bằng thuyền — 'by + phương tiện' diễn tả cách di chuyển (by boat, by car, by train)
người phụ nữ mệt mỏi — tính từ đứng trước danh từ để bổ nghĩa (tired = mệt mỏi)
không ra ngoài — cụm động từ: come out = đi ra / xuất hiện
súp cá (colocation: fish + soup — món ăn đặc trưng)
mì pasta (món ăn Ý phổ biến)
đảo Croatia (danh từ + tính từ quốc tịch: Croatian = thuộc Croatia)

grammarNotes.title: Indefinite frequency: 'sometimes', 'ever', 'never'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng 'sometimes' (đôi khi) cho việc xảy ra không thường xuyên. 'Ever' (đã bao giờ) chỉ dùng trong câu hỏi và phủ định. 'Never' (không bao giờ) cho phủ định mạnh. Trong bài: 'Sometimes the keeper asks for pasta.' 'Sometimes the electrician asks for nothing.' 'I asked Ivana if she ever cooks something different.' (câu hỏi gián tiếp với 'ever'). 'Sometimes' và 'never' đứng trước động từ chính; 'sometimes' cũng có thể đứng đầu câu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses 'sometimes' for occasional events. 'Ever' (have you ever...?) appears in questions and negatives only. 'Never' is a strong negative. The passage: 'Sometimes the keeper asks for pasta.' 'I asked Ivana if she ever cooks something different.' 'Sometimes' and 'never' usually sit before the main verb; 'sometimes' can also start a sentence.

translationVi:
Tôi tên là Tom. Tôi là một nhà báo từ London. Mùa đông năm ngoái tôi đã dành mười ngày ở một ngọn hải đăng nhỏ trên một hòn đảo Croatia. Hải đăng có một người trông coi, một thợ điện và một đầu bếp. Tôi đến để viết về người trông coi. Tôi rốt cuộc viết về người đầu bếp.

Đầu bếp là một phụ nữ tên Ivana. Cô năm mươi ba tuổi. Cô đã làm đầu bếp ở hải đăng mười sáu năm. Cô đến bằng thuyền vào thứ Hai. Cô rời đi vào thứ Bảy. Cô nấu cho ba người.

Đêm đầu, bữa tối là súp cá, bánh mì, pho mát và một ly rượu vang. Đêm thứ hai, cũng vậy. Đêm thứ ba, cũng vậy. Tôi hỏi Ivana có bao giờ cô nấu món khác không.

Cô cười. Cô nói: 'Mấy người đàn ông bảo tôi nấu súp cá. Tôi nấu súp cá. Đôi khi anh trông coi muốn ăn mì. Tôi nấu mì. Đôi khi anh thợ điện không yêu cầu gì — anh ấy buồn — và tôi vẫn nấu súp cá. Người đàn ông buồn nên ăn súp.'

Tôi hỏi: 'Còn cho chính cô?'

Cô đáp: 'Tôi ăn cùng món. Nếu tôi nấu hai món, tôi là một người phụ nữ mệt. Nếu tôi nấu một món, tôi là một đầu bếp.'

Ngày thứ mười, tôi rời đi. Người trông coi tiễn tôi. Anh thợ điện vẫy tay. Ivana ở trong bếp. Cô không ra. Súp đang ở trên bếp.

questions.prompt: Who did he go to write about? Who did he end up writing about?
options: Went for the keeper; wrote about the cook
Went for the cook; wrote about the keeper
Went for the electrician; wrote about the boat
questions.explanation: 'I went to write about the keeper. I ended up writing about the cook.'

questions.prompt: How long has Ivana been the cook?
When does she come and leave?
questions.explanation: 'She comes by boat on Mondays. She leaves on Saturdays.'

questions.prompt: What was dinner on the first three nights?
options: Fish soup, bread, cheese, and a glass of wine — the same each night

questions.prompt: What does Ivana cook for the sad electrician?
options: Fish soup anyway — 'sad men should eat soup.'
questions.explanation: 'I cook fish soup anyway. Sad men should eat soup.'

questions.prompt: Why does she cook only one meal?
options: If she cooks two, she is a tired woman; if she cooks one, she is a cook.
She is not allowed to cook two.

questions.prompt: What happened on the tenth day, when Tom left?
options: The keeper saw him off; the electrician waved; Ivana was in the kitchen and did not come out; the soup was on the stove.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: The person who keeps a place running is sometimes the one nobody came to write about — and her rule is simpler than anyone's.
Croatian food is the best in Europe.
questions.explanation: The reframing of the article and Ivana's 'one meal' rule.
questions.hint: Notice who the article is actually about.

cloze.template:
My name is Tom. I am a {{b1}} from London. Last winter I spent ten days at a small lighthouse on a Croatian island. The lighthouse has a keeper, an electrician, and a {{b2}}. I went to write about the keeper. I ended up writing about the cook.

The cook is a woman named Ivana. She is fifty-three. She has been the cook at the lighthouse for {{b3}} years. She comes by boat on Mondays. She leaves on Saturdays. She cooks for three.

On my first night, dinner was fish {{b4}}, bread, cheese, and one glass of wine. On the second night, the same. On the third night, the same. I asked Ivana if she {{b5}} cooks something different.

She smiled. She said, 'The men ask me to cook fish soup. I cook fish soup. Sometimes the keeper asks for pasta. I cook pasta. Sometimes the {{b6}} asks for nothing — he is sad — and I cook fish soup anyway. Sad men should {{b7}} soup.'

I said, 'And for yourself?'

She said, 'I eat the same. If I cook two meals, I am a tired woman. If I cook one meal, I am a {{b8}}.'

On the tenth day, I left. The keeper saw me off. The electrician {{b9}}. Ivana was in the kitchen. She did not come out. The soup was on the {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: Sometimes the electrician asks for nothing.
If she cooks one, she is a cook.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Ivana says 'If I cook two meals, I am a tired woman. If I cook one meal, I am a cook.' Is there a place in your work or family where you have been quietly running two systems instead of one — and could collapse them into one without losing what mattered? What would the one version look like?
